verdict_reasoning: KLK2 is a serine protease of the kallikrein family, primarily expressed in prostatic tissue as an intracellular enzyme. It is a secreted protein (signal peptide-driven, no TM domain) that functions in the intracellular milieu and extracellular prostate fluid to activate pro-PSA. While KLK2 is detected in blood/serum as a consequence of tissue damage, shedding, or secretion into prostate fluid, it has no stable surface anchoring mechanism — no TM domain, no GPI anchor, no documented covalent tethering to a surface TM partner, and no wash-resistant PM association. The protein is not a surface receptor and does not have a documented non-baseline ecto-pool in any contextual scenario (no cancer-induced surface display, no cell-death-induced externalization, no tissue-restricted surface display, no lysosomal-exocytosis role, no dual-localization minority PM pool). It is a classic secreted serine protease that reaches the extracellular space via constitutive secretion, not surface anchoring.
